worth_it_explanation: Ad quality scanning is the layer that keeps malware, auto-redirects, and policy-violating creatives off a publisher's pages, so it protects both the audience and the advertiser trust the inventory depends on. A creative that hijacks the page or carries malware costs reader goodwill that is slow to rebuild, and demand partners pull spend from supply that lets bad creatives through. The check is a review of the scanning and blocking controls a publisher already has access to through Magnite, so it costs reading and reporting time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete control the publisher can turn on, tighten, or act on.
intro: Creative quality scanning is the screening that sits between programmatic demand and a publisher's pages, checking each creative for malware, forced redirects, and policy violations before and after it serves. This subcategory is about the scanning controls themselves and what happens when a creative fails them, rather than the brand-suitability category blocks that decide which advertiser verticals are allowed at all. These checks confirm that scanning is switched on across the supply a publisher runs through Magnite, and that a flagged creative is blocked and kept blocked rather than left active while it keeps serving.

audit_checks.title: Creative scanning for malware and forced redirects is not enabled across all demand
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A malicious creative that serves through unscanned supply can hijack the page with a forced redirect, deliver malware, or push a deceptive offer, which costs reader trust directly and can trigger browser or demand-partner penalties against the publisher's domain. Because the damage hits at the scale of however many impressions the creative serves before anyone notices, leaving any slice of demand unscanned is a gap that can cause harm well beyond the revenue from that slice.

educate.base:
Programmatic demand brings creatives onto a publisher's pages from many buyers at once, and not all of them are safe. A small share carry malware, force a redirect that takes the reader off the page without a click, auto-play in ways that break the layout, or run a deceptive offer that violates content policy. Creative quality scanning is the control that screens for these before and after a creative serves, so the goal of this check is to confirm that the screening covers every path demand takes onto the page rather than a subset.

The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Tech Lab frames ad quality review as a process where automated systems or analysts inspect the creative, its landing page, its tags, and its behavior to confirm the creative is free of malware, complies with content policy, and functions correctly. Magnite applies scanning of this kind to the demand it routes, and a publisher running supply through Magnite controls which protections are active and how strict they are. The risk is not that scanning is missing entirely. The risk is that it covers some demand paths and not others, so a creative blocked on one path still reaches readers through another.

Defense against malicious creatives works best in layers. The IAB Tech Lab Ad Management API standard describes malware detection and policy compliance as part of the creative approval step, and Prebid's guidance adds creative isolation through a SafeFrame so a creative that does slip through cannot reach the rest of the page. A publisher can pair the scanning Magnite provides with SafeFrame rendering in the page setup, so the two layers cover each other.

A few signs point to scanning that does not cover all demand:
  • Scanning or creative-quality controls are switched on for some demand paths, such as open-auction supply, but not for direct deals, private marketplace deals, or a separate header-bidding wrapper
  • No record exists of which demand paths the scanning covers and which it does not
  • Reader or advertiser reports of redirects or malware have surfaced for inventory the publisher believed was screened
You may want to confirm that creative scanning covers every path demand takes onto the page. One approach is to list each demand path running through Magnite, check whether scanning and creative-quality controls are active on each, and close any path that is serving creatives unscreened.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a forced redirect or a malware creative lands on readers who came for the content and reads as the publisher's failure, not the advertiser's, so the trust cost is direct. You may want to confirm scanning covers the placements that carry the most reader attention first, such as article-body and homepage units, and pair it with SafeFrame rendering so a creative that slips through cannot reach the surrounding page.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a deceptive or redirecting creative can pull a shopper away from a purchase path and undercut the trust the site depends on to convert, so the cost shows up in lost sales as well as reader goodwill. You may want to confirm scanning covers the product and category pages where buying intent is highest because a hijacked session there is more costly than one on an editorial page.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed configuration documentation for its ad quality controls is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review which demand paths run through Magnite, then confirm whether creative scanning and quality controls are active on each, including direct deals, private marketplace deals, and any separate wrapper.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console and Demand Manager UI
steps: Open the Magnite console (the SpringServe or Demand Manager interface the publisher uses) and list every demand path active on the inventory, including open auction, private marketplace deals, and direct deals.
For each path, locate the creative-quality or ad-quality settings and record whether scanning for malware, forced redirects, and policy violations is switched on.
Check any separate header-bidding wrapper the publisher runs alongside Magnite and record whether its demand passes through the same scanning or bypasses it.
Record any demand path where scanning is off or its status cannot be confirmed. That is the path to close.

magnite_reporting.label: Magnite reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by demand path or deal, and look for creative-quality or blocked-creative metrics if the reporting exposes them.
Cross-reference any reader or advertiser reports of redirects, malware, or broken creatives against the demand path that served them.
Note whether the reporting confirms scanning is active on each path or whether a path shows served creatives with no scanning record.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per demand path running through Magnite, whether creative scanning for malware, forced redirects, and policy violations is active. A setup where every path, including direct deals, private marketplace deals, and any separate wrapper, passes through scanning passes. A setup where one or more demand paths serve creatives unscreened, or where the scanning status of a path cannot be confirmed, is a fail recorded with the paths affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether creative scanning is active for each demand path above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs creative scanning on some demand paths through Magnite but not all, so creatives can reach the page unscreened through a path the scanning does not cover. A malicious creative that serves through an unscanned path can force a redirect, deliver malware, or push a deceptive offer, and the cost lands on reader trust and the publisher's standing with demand partners rather than on the buyer. {{voice_recommend}} listing every demand path active through Magnite, including direct deals, private marketplace deals, and any separate header-bidding wrapper, confirming creative scanning is switched on for each, and pairing it with SafeFrame rendering so a creative that does slip through cannot reach the surrounding page. After the change, recheck the demand paths to confirm none are serving creatives unscreened.

audit_checks.title: Flagged creatives are not blocked and kept blocked after a quality failure
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Scanning that flags a bad creative does little good if the creative stays active afterward because it keeps serving to readers until someone intervenes. A flagged creative left unblocked, or one blocked then allowed back through a different deal, keeps the harm running, so the gap is in the response to a flag rather than in the detection.

educate.base:
Detecting a bad creative is only half of ad quality. The other half is the response, which is whether a flagged creative is blocked, how quickly, and whether it stays blocked rather than reappearing through another demand path. A scan that flags a forced-redirect creative but leaves it serving has caught the problem without stopping it, so the creative keeps reaching readers until a person acts on the flag.

The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Tech Lab Ad Management API standard treats approval and disapproval as a two-way exchange, where the supply platform records a verdict on a creative and the buyer receives the feedback. For a publisher, the audit question is what happens on the supply side after a creative is disapproved. A block applied to one deal but not the publisher's whole account can let the same creative serve again through a different buyer or a private marketplace deal. A block applied manually with no standing rule can lapse, so a creative that was stopped last week serves again this week through a new auction.

Acting on a flag means three things working together. First, a flagged creative is blocked at the account level so it cannot serve through any path, not just the one that caught it. Second, the block holds over time rather than expiring or needing to be reapplied. Third, there is a record of what was flagged and what was done, so a recurring offender or a buyer that repeatedly submits bad creatives can be spotted and addressed at the source.

A few signs point to flags that are not acted on:
  • Creatives flagged by scanning remain active, or are blocked on one deal while still serving on another
  • No account-level block list or creative-control record exists that holds across demand paths
  • The same creative or the same buyer is flagged repeatedly with no escalation or source-level action
You may want to confirm that a flagged creative is blocked across every demand path and stays blocked. One approach is to review recent creative flags, check whether each was blocked account-wide and remained blocked, and set a standing rule so the block holds rather than relying on a manual step each time.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a creative flagged on one section can keep serving in another if the block is scoped to a single deal rather than the account, so the same bad creative reaches readers elsewhere on the site. You may want to confirm flags trigger an account-wide block and that a record of repeat offenders feeds back to the demand source.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a deceptive creative flagged once can resurface through a different deal and undercut shopper trust again, so a block that holds across every demand path matters more where a hijacked session costs a sale. You may want to confirm the block is account-level and durable rather than a one-off action on the deal that first caught it.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed documentation for blocking creatives and managing block lists is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review recent creative flags and what was done with each, then confirm that a flagged creative is blocked across every demand path and that the block holds over time.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console and Demand Manager UI
steps: Open the creative-control or block-list section of the Magnite console and review the creatives flagged or disapproved over the recent reporting period.
For each flagged creative, record whether it was blocked at the account level or only on the single deal that caught it, and whether it can still serve through another path.
Check whether the block is a standing rule that holds over time or a manual action that could lapse and let the creative serve again.
Look for any buyer or creative flagged more than once and record whether an escalation or source-level action followed.

magnite_reporting.label: Magnite reporting
steps: Build a report covering blocked or disapproved creatives over time, if the reporting exposes that data, and check whether any flagged creative continued to serve after it was flagged.
Cross-reference repeat flags against the buyer or deal that submitted them to spot a recurring source.
Note any flagged creative that kept serving. That points to a flag that was detected but not acted on.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for the recent creative flags, whether each flagged creative was blocked across every demand path and whether the block held rather than lapsing or being scoped to a single deal. A setup where flagged creatives are blocked account-wide, the blocks hold over time, and repeat offenders are escalated to the source passes. A setup where flagged creatives kept serving, were blocked on one path while active on another, or relied on a manual step that lapsed is a fail recorded with the creatives or buyers affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record what was done with each flagged creative and whether the block held above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} flags bad creatives through scanning but does not always block them and keep them blocked, so a flagged creative can keep serving or reappear through a different demand path. Detection without a durable, account-wide block leaves the harm running because the creative reaches readers until someone acts on the flag, and a block scoped to one deal lets the same creative serve again through another. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the recent creative flags, blocking each flagged creative at the account level so it cannot serve through any path, setting the block as a standing rule that holds over time, and escalating any buyer that repeatedly submits bad creatives back to the source. After the change, recheck the recent flags to confirm none kept serving once they were caught.
